core_intuitions: The synchronous RL barrier wastes the fleet: lock-step gen->train->sync leaves each side idle while the other works, and heavy-tailed response lengths add a straggler tax where the whole batch waits on its single longest sample.

Async off-policy RL breaks the barrier with a rollout queue + versioned weight store: generators sample continuously with the weights they hold, the trainer consumes rollouts and republishes weights every N steps, and every rollout is stamped with a policy version so staleness s = k - v is bounded and correctable.

Off-policy data forces importance sampling: reweight each token by rho_t = pi_theta / pi_theta_old, but the sequence-level product of per-token ratios has unbounded variance and must be tamed by PPO clipping, truncated importance sampling, and dropping wildly off-policy rollouts.

There are two DISTINCT ratios: the staleness ratio (current vs older policy) AND the engine-mismatch ratio (inference engine vs training engine computing different log-probs for the SAME weights) — the latter is the silent async bug that biases the ratio away from 1 even at zero staleness.

Decentralization is possible precisely because async is loosely coupled: only prompts, rollouts, and infrequent weight broadcasts cross the WAN, none latency-critical; stateless generation goes to permissionless edge GPUs while the stateful trainer stays centralized.

Untrusted workers create two attacks — reward forgery (killed by recomputing rewards on a trusted verifier) and inference forgery (caught by TOPLOC, a locality-sensitive commitment to top-k activations, cheaply re-verified with a batched prefill: robust to benign GPU numerics, sensitive to a real model swap).

existing.name: primerl-sync-vs-async-timeline
existing.issue: Reads cleanly in both themes. Two stacked Gantt lanes make the idle gaps in sync and the continuous busy in async immediately legible; the straggler annotation lands the tax intuition. Only nit is the small 'gen idle (waiting on weights theta_k)' label crowding the straggler arrow, but it stays legible.

existing.name: primerl-queue-async-architecture
existing.issue: Clear closed loop prompts -> inference workers -> rollout queue -> trainer -> weight store -> back, with the policy-version stamp called out as the key bookkeeping. Works in both themes. Minor: the 'tagged with policy version v / push finished rollouts' label slightly overlaps its arrow in dark mode, and the central bookkeeping box's staleness subtext is tiny, but both remain readable.

existing.name: primerl-decentralized-trust-pipeline
existing.issue: Strong in light mode (WAN boundary, trusted vs untrusted halves, TOPLOC+verifier gate) but has dark-mode problems: the bottom 'WAN boundary — only prompts out, rollouts in, weight broadcasts cross' caption is clipped against the bottom edge and goes faint; the 'theta_k broadcast (sharded / BitTorrent fan-out)' label overlaps the dashed fan-out arrows and is hard to read; and the two info boxes ('anyone can join...', 'weight broadcast...') stay hardcoded white in dark mode, which is theme-unsafe and jarring.
existing.improvement: Add bottom padding inside the viewBox so the WAN-boundary caption is never clipped, and give it a theme-aware fill. Put the 'theta_k broadcast' label on a small solid theme-aware chip so it reads over the dashed arrows in both themes. Recolor the two white info boxes to theme-aware surface fills with theme-aware text so they render correctly in dark mode.

new_figures.name: offpolicy-importance-ratio-correction
new_figures.concept: Why the off-policy sequence importance ratio explodes and how PPO clipping + truncated IS + dropping tame it, plus the two distinct ratios (staleness vs engine-mismatch).
new_figures.rationale: This is the algorithmic heart of the chapter and it is entirely unserved by any figure. Readers grasp 'stamp a version and correct for staleness' abstractly but not WHY a product of per-token ratios each near 1 blows up, nor the subtle point that the numerator is the trainer-recomputed differentiable policy while the denominator is the inference-engine-logged behavior policy — and that a SECOND, separate ratio (inference vs training engine on the same weights) biases things even at zero staleness. A single static infographic makes the multiplicative-compounding intuition and the three defenses click in a way the equations alone do not.
new_figures.placement: Section 'The math of staleness: importance sampling for off-policy LLM RL', anchored right after the three-defenses list (PPO clipping / truncated IS / sequence-level masking) and before the from-scratch code block.
new_figures.brief:
A three-panel static infographic, theme-safe (neutral surfaces, one cool accent for 'stable/kept', one warm accent for 'outlier/dropped'; no hardcoded page colors; small illustrative integers only, invent no benchmark numbers).

PANEL 1 — 'Why the ratio explodes' (top, widest): Draw a short token sequence o_1 ... o_T as ~8 cells left-to-right (label the row 'one response, tokens'). Under each token draw a small vertical bar centered on a dashed horizontal baseline marked '1.0', representing per-token rho_t = pi_theta(o_t)/pi_theta_old(o_t). Make MOST bars sit right at ~1.0 (barely above/below the line) and TWO of them clearly deviate (one noticeably tall, one short). To the right of the row, an equals/arrow to a single tall skinny bar labeled 'sequence ratio = product of rho_t' that shoots far off the top of the panel (add a jagged break-line to signal 'off the chart'), captioned 'tiny per-token gaps COMPOUND multiplicatively -> variance unbounded'. The visual takeaway: near-1 ratios multiplied over a long sequence balloon.

PANEL 2 — 'The ratio has two independent sources of drift' (middle): A compact side-by-side. Left half labels the ratio's two sides: NUMERATOR pi_theta 'recomputed by the TRAINER on current weights — MUST be differentiable' (small chip icon of a gradient/backprop), DENOMINATOR pi_theta_old 'LOGGED once by the INFERENCE engine at generation, travels with the rollout' (small chip icon of a stored log). This is the STALENESS ratio. Right half, boxed and flagged with a warning glyph, shows the SECOND ratio: 'engine mismatch — inference engine (fp8, fused kernels) vs training engine (bf16, FSDP) on the SAME weights & tokens' with a tiny inequality 'pi^infer != pi^train, so ratio != 1 even at s = 0 (the silent bug)'. Keep the two clearly separated so the reader sees they are different phenomena.

PANEL 3 — 'Three clamps that tame it' (bottom, a row of three): (1) 'PPO clip' — the per-token bars from Panel 1 now squeezed into a shaded band [1-eps, 1+eps] around 1.0, outliers flattened to the band edge, caption 'bounds how far each token can move'. (2) 'Truncated IS (cap C)' — a bar rising into a horizontal ceiling line labeled 'C (typ. 2-10)' that chops the behavior-side ratio, caption 'caps engine-mismatch outliers'. (3) 'Drop' — a whole rollout tile whose 'sequence ratio' badge is outside a 'trust band', shown crossed out / routed to a trash chip, caption 'wildly off-policy -> discard, more noise than signal'. 

Reading order strictly top-to-bottom, left-to-right. All labels legible at figure size; nothing overlaps. Must be fully understandable as a single static frame.

new_figures.name: toploc-verification-mechanism
new_figures.concept: TOPLOC verifies an untrusted GPU actually ran the claimed model via a locality-sensitive commitment to top-k activations, cheaply re-checked with a batched prefill — robust to benign numerics, sensitive to a real model swap.
new_figures.rationale: TOPLOC is the chapter's most novel and most counterintuitive mechanism (locality-sensitive, NOT cryptographic hashing of activations) and no figure serves it. The prose asks the reader to hold several subtle ideas at once: commit to top-k dominant components not exact floats; verify with a cheap parallel prefill instead of re-generating; and accept within a tolerance because honest hardware disagrees in the low bits while a forged run diverges in the dominant structure. A staged static infographic that literally shows the top-k overlap passing under benign noise and failing under a model swap makes the 'robust yet discriminative' idea concrete in one look.
new_figures.placement: Section 'TOPLOC: verifying that an untrusted GPU really ran the model', subsection 'How TOPLOC works (the mechanism)', anchored after the 3-step numbered mechanism list and before the toploc_commit/toploc_verify code block.
new_figures.brief:
A left-to-right three-stage static infographic with a two-case comparison at the end. Theme-safe (neutral surfaces, one cool accent = PASS/consistent, one warm accent = FAIL/diverged; no hardcoded page background colors; small illustrative integers only — draw top-k as top-4 highlighted out of ~12 cells purely for legibility, and DO NOT invent benchmark numbers).

STAGE 1 — PROVER (untrusted worker), labeled 'runs during generation on the cheap external GPU': For one generated token, draw its hidden-state vector at the committed layer as a horizontal row of ~12 cells with varying heights (a few clearly tall 'spike' cells). Highlight the top-4 largest-MAGNITUDE cells (outline + accent). From those, form a compact 'commitment' chip listing {indices, coarse-quantized values, signs} with a size tag 'a few bytes / token'. Show the commitment traveling ALONGSIDE the completion (a small 'completion + proof' packet) toward the trusted side. Caption the core idea: 'commit to the DOMINANT components, not the exact floats'.

STAGE 2 — VERIFIER (trusted), labeled 'recompute cheaply': Contrast two compute shapes explicitly. Small icon A 'prover: autoregressive generation — T sequential forwards, KV cache GROWS' (draw a staircase of T steps with an expanding cache block). Small icon B 'verifier: ONE batched prefill — all T tokens in parallel, single big matmul' (draw one wide block over T tokens). Bold caption between them: 'verification is MUCH cheaper than generation -> trainer can afford to check every rollout'. The verifier recomputes the same top-4 features for the token.

STAGE 3 — ROBUST COMPARE (two stacked cases sharing one layout): Show the prover's top-4 index set vs the verifier's top-4 index set as two small labeled sets with their overlap. 
  CASE A 'honest worker, different hardware (A100 vs H100)': benign low-bit noise nudges heights and swaps a near-tie, but the dominant cells are the same -> sets mostly overlap (>= tolerance) -> big PASS badge (cool accent), caption 'low bits perturbed, top-k structure preserved'. 
  CASE B 'forged: smaller model / wrong precision / fabricated tokens': the activation geometry shifts, different cells dominate -> sets barely overlap (< tolerance) -> FAIL badge (warm accent), caption 'geometry moved, top-k diverges -> reject (and penalize)'. 

Bottom strip — one-line thesis banner: 'Locality-sensitive, NOT cryptographic: a SHA of activations would fail every honest run; hash a quantity STABLE under benign perturbation instead.' 

Strict left-to-right reading order with the two cases stacked at the right. No overlaps; every label legible; fully understandable as a static frame.

summary: The chapter is well-served on the systems layer: the sync-vs-async timeline and the queue architecture are keeps (clear in both themes), and the decentralized trust pipeline is a refine (dark-mode caption clipping, an overlapping broadcast label, and two theme-unsafe white info boxes). The two genuinely foundational, figure-poor intuitions are the off-policy importance-sampling correction (why a product of near-1 per-token ratios explodes; the two distinct ratios of staleness vs engine-mismatch; the clip/TIS/drop defenses) and the TOPLOC mechanism (locality-sensitive top-k activation commitment, cheap batched-prefill verification, robust-yet-discriminative comparison). I propose one static infographic for each — both priority-1 — and no long-animations, since a single well-staged frame conveys each mechanism.
